title: C(A, B, Θ) → E — the fundamental operator
summary: The book's root equation. Appendix A.1 defines the coordination function C: A × B × Θ → E, where two elements coordinate in a context to produce emergence that neither could produce alone. Every other diagram in this book — Hardware/Software/Knowware, Facebook three-body, Netflix, AlphaFold, Pearl's Ladder, π/φ balance — is an instantiation of this single operator with different (A, B, Θ) bindings. The crucial property: E ≠ f(A) ∪ f(B). Emergence cannot be decomposed back into individual contributions.
source: Appendix A.1 — "C: A × B × Θ → E, where E ∉ {f(A) ∪ f(B)} for any functions f"

nodes.label: Element A
nodes.description: First coordinating element, state space S_A

nodes.label: Element B
nodes.description: Second coordinating element, state space S_B

nodes.label: Context Θ
nodes.description: The third body — environmental and relational parameters that shape coordination

nodes.label: Coordination Function C
nodes.description: The operator C: A × B × Θ → E. Not commutative; not decomposable.

nodes.label: Emergence E
nodes.description: The output. E ≠ f(A) ∪ f(B). Irreducible to individual contributions.

edges.description: Θ shapes how A and B compose — changes the operator itself
Emergence shifts the context — this is what makes coordination evolve

identifiability_note: Meta-diagram: the operator itself is defined, not measured. Identifiability belongs to each specialization (HW/SW/Knowware, Facebook, Netflix, etc.), which bind A, B, Θ to specific observable variables and inherit identifiability from that binding.
